summary: DHH explains why Fortnite is his family's favorite game, arguing it teaches kids valuable life skills through cooperative squad play. He enforces two simple house rules — stay together and no complaining — which teach teamwork and emotional regulation. Unlike competitive family games like Mario Kart, Fortnite puts the whole family on the same team working toward shared objectives. He concludes that not all screen time is equal, and that well-chosen games can be remarkably effective teaching tools.
key_points: Two simple rules — stay together and no complaining — teach cooperative discipline and emotional regulation
Fortnite's team-based structure makes it superior to competitive family games for teaching collaboration
Learning to lose gracefully and temper frustration are life skills that transfer well beyond gaming
Screen time quality varies enormously — cooperative gaming can be genuinely educational
money_quotes: All screen time is not created equal.
If you get all pissy and blamey when you're knocked out, the session ends, because nobody wants to listen to that (especially dad!).
If you want to play more, and get better, you better start tempering your frustrations. Wonderful life skill to develop early.
It's ever-so tempting to stray from the squad to chase your own goal of getting better gear, and it comes easy to blame your brother when the other team gets you.
Unlike something like Mario Kart or Smash Brothers, which pits the family against itself (also fun, but less learning!), Fortnite puts us all on the same team, striving for the same objective.
key_insight: Cooperative video games with enforced rules about teamwork and emotional control can be powerful tools for teaching children discipline, collaboration, and resilience.
